evidence_quotes.quote: publishers deactivating game servers at will

paragraphs.text: Subject: Strong Support for Including Stop Killing Games in the Digital Fairness Act Proposal Dear European Commission, I write as an EU citizen deeply encouraged by the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) initiative. This proposal promises essential consumer protections for the digital environmentaddressing dark patterns, manipulative design, unfair contracts, influencer marketing, addictive features, and algorithmic profiling. It represents a timely evolution of digital consumer rights in an increasingly complex online world. However, I urge that the DFA explicitly extend its protections to digital video gamesa sector glaringly overlooked but critically in need of legal safeguards. The StopKillingGames initiative highlights a sweeping consumer rights issue: publishers deactivating game servers at will, rendering purchased games utterly unplayable and effectively destroying all working copies postpurchase. Its European Citizens Initiative has gained over 1.4 million signatures, surpassing the threshold for Commission consideration. This level of support demonstrates widespread concern: consumers are willing to pay for games, but not to lose their access through no fault of their own or lack of alternatives. Why this matters: Ownership vs. Rent: Consumers purchase games under the illusion of ownership. When access can be revoked anytime by shutting servers, the concept of ownership" collapses. This contradicts basic consumer rights. Cultural Preservation: Digital games represent a significant part of cultural heritage. If unaddressed, many titlesespecially online-only gamesrisk disappearing forever. A policy that mandates playable end-of-life solutions (offline modes or private-server handover) would enable preservation and continued enjoyment. Consumer Fairness & Market Integrity: The practice of shutting off games undermines trust in digital markets. Consumers deserve transparency and fair termsespecially when spending money on products that might vanish without notice. I appreciate concerns expressed by industry bodies, such as Video Games Europe, about costs or development burden. However, the intent of StopKillingGames is not to mandate indefinite server support. Rather, it seeks reasonable transitional mechanisms: an offline patch or permission for private serversdesigned in consultation with developers to avoid undue burden. Concrete suggestions to strengthen the DFA proposal: Include digital video games explicitly among the categories of digital products to be regulated. Require that publishers selling or licensing games in the EU implement endofsupport transition plans, including: A version of the game that continues to function offline post server shutdown; OR an allowed mechanism for consumer-hosted private servers. Ensure transparency in licensing agreements (EULAs) to clarify users rights and what rights they lose when servers cease operations. Protect innovation and preserve developer choice by allowing flexibility in how game preservation is achievedwhile ensuring fair outcomes for consumers. The StopKillingGames campaign is supported by players, consumer advocates, content creators (including PewDiePie, xQc, Louis Rossmann), and even studios such as Owlcat Games. It is backed by politicians in the European Parliament, like NicolaeȘtefănuță. In overlooking video games, the DFA would miss a bold opportunity to safeguard digital product fairness across one of the fastest growing creative industries. Including game preservation measures would ensure that EU citizens dont lose access overnight to what theyve legally acquired. I strongly support the DFA, and call upon the Commission to integrate StopKillingGames principles into its draft legislationgrounding it in the lived experience of digital consumers. This would uphold consumer trust, cultural continuity, market integrity, and fairness in the digital single market. Thank you for considering this essential addition.
